description: The image shows only the CrossMark publication-integrity logo—a red bookmark icon inside a blue circular border with the adjacent text label 'CrossMark'—and contains no materials-science experimental data. No micrographs, diffraction patterns, spectra, stress-strain curves, phase diagrams, or schematic illustrations of alloy microstructures are visible, so no material system, sample designation, heat-treatment condition, phase identification, or performance metric can be extracted directly from the pixels. The surrounding paper context identifies the work as a review on Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels covering ferritic, duplex, and austenitic alloy classes, deformation mechanisms, and processing strategies, but this specific image does not depict any of those metallurgical findings. Because the figure caption is empty and no citation sentences are provided, no direct scientific observation or quantitative trend can be attributed to this figure.

Figure 1 comprises two line graphs illustrating the effect of alloying elements on the physical properties of ferritic iron. Subfigure (a) plots density (g/cm³) versus alloy content (wt%) for ferritic iron alloyed with C, Al, Si, Mn, and Cr. Carbon causes the sharpest drop, reducing density to roughly 7.0 g/cm³ at only ~2 wt%, while Al yields a strong linear decrease to about 6.1 g/cm³ at 16 wt%; Si produces a moderate reduction, and Mn and Cr leave the density nearly unchanged. Subfigure (b) shows Young's modulus (GPa) of annealed Fe-Al steels versus Al content, compiling curves from Frommeyer [24], Kubo [28], Yamamoto-Taniguchi [28], and Fukuroi-Shibuya [28]. All sources report a steady decline from ~215 GPa toward 120–160 GPa as Al rises to 16 wt%. The caption and text state a loss of 2–2.5% per 1 wt% Al, caused by reduced lattice energy and increased Fe-Al atomic spacing. Collectively, the figure highlights that Al effectively decreases density but simultaneously degrades elastic stiffness, a key disadvantage for low-density steel development.

Figure 2 presents the Young's modulus of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels as a function of manganese content (5–40 wt% Mn), measured by the resonance method. The plot compares three processing conditions: a red solid line for as-hot rolled Fe-(15–28)Mn-(7–10)Al-(0.7–1.2)C steels showing the highest modulus (~168–180 GPa); a hatched band bounded by black solid lines for as-cold rolled and annealed steels of the same alloy system with intermediate values (~158–166 GPa); and a blue solid line for as-cast Fe-(5–40)Mn-10Al-1C steels exhibiting the lowest modulus (~143–152 GPa). The as-cast state shows a pronounced drop in E with increasing Mn up to ~18 wt% before plateauing, whereas the processed states display a broad peak around 10–12 wt% Mn followed by a gradual decrease. The caption and text explain that micro-cavities and segregation in cast structures reduce the modulus, while reheating and hot rolling heal these defects, raising E. Cold deformation and annealing slightly decrease E again due to texture effects. Overall, all Fe-Mn-Al-C variants exhibit a Young's modulus roughly 20% lower than conventional HSLA grades (~210 GPa).

Figure 3 presents a grouped bar chart comparing the specific stiffness of four materials—Aluminium (6xxx series), HSLA steel, Fe-8Al ferritic steel, and Fe-22Mn-8Al-0.8C austenitic steel—normalized against that of aluminium across three part-dependent indices: E/ρ for a tie (n = 1), √E/ρ for a beam (n = 1/2), and ∛E/ρ for a panel (n = 1/3). The y-axis denotes specific stiffness relative to aluminium. For the tie index, HSLA and aluminium exhibit the highest values (~1.0–1.03), followed by Fe-8Al (~0.93) and Fe-22Mn-8Al-0.8C (~0.87). Under the beam index, aluminium remains at 1.0, while HSLA and Fe-8Al drop to ~0.61, and Fe-22Mn-8Al-0.8C falls to ~0.58. For the panel index, aluminium again leads at 1.0, with Fe-8Al (~0.53), HSLA (~0.51), and Fe-22Mn-8Al-0.8C (~0.51) clustering at lower values. The figure demonstrates that the low-density steels do not surpass HSLA or aluminium in specific stiffness for any structural configuration. This supports the conclusion that weight-saving applications of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels must target components where stiffness is not the critical design constraint.

This figure presents the Fe-rich portion of the binary Fe-Al phase diagram adapted from Kubaschewski [88], serving as the foundational equilibrium reference for ferritic Fe-Al steels. The temperature-composition map spans 0–40 wt% Al and 200–1600 °C, delineating phase boundaries for liquid (L), austenite (γ), disordered body-centered cubic α-ferrite (A2), ordered B2 FeAl, ordered DO₃ Fe₃Al, FeAl+ε, and FeAl₂. Key invariant temperatures explicitly labeled include 1536 °C (Fe melting point), 1392 °C, 1310 °C, 1215 °C, 1092 °C, 911 °C (γ to α transformation), and 552 °C. A dashed line indicates the magnetic transformation extending from the Fe-rich side toward ~13 wt% Al, while the K-state (short-range order) domain is enclosed by solubility limits K₁ and K₂ within roughly 10–24 wt% Al below ~550 °C. The diagram supports the core conclusion that the disorder-order transition from BCC_A2 to BCC_B2 initiates at Al contents above ~2%, with the transition temperature increasing as Al content rises, thereby promoting ordered phase formation within the ferrite matrix.

Figure 5 presents three vertical-section phase diagrams comparing the Fe–C binary system with the Fe–15Mn–C and Fe–7Al–C pseudo-binary systems, illustrating how Mn and Al additions alter phase stabilities and fields relevant to low-density steel design. Panel (a) shows the baseline Fe–C diagram with ferrite (α, δ), austenite (γ), cementite (M3C), and the invariant points S (eutectoid) and E (maximum C solubility in γ). Panel (b) demonstrates that 15 wt% Mn stabilizes γ to lower temperatures, shifts the peritectic reaction toward lower C, depresses the S-point temperature and C content, and introduces M7C3 and M23C6 carbides at lower temperatures alongside M3C. Panel (c) reveals that 7 wt% Al enlarges the high-temperature peritectic range, extends the (δ+γ) field, raises the S- and E-point temperatures and C concentrations, replaces part of the (γ+M3C) field with (γ+κ) and (γ+κ+M3C) regions (where κ is kappa carbide), and introduces an A2-to-B2 ordering transition in ferrite indicated by the dashed line. Together the diagrams establish that Mn and Al dramatically reshape phase equilibria in Fe–C, governing κ-carbide formation, ferrite ordering, and austenite stability in Fe–Mn–Al–C steels.

Figure 6 presents a set of crystal structure schematics illustrating the atomic arrangement evolution in Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic alloys. Panel (a) shows the unit cell of disordered γ-austenite, where Fe/Mn and Al atoms are randomly distributed at the face-centered cubic lattice sites. Panel (b) depicts the ordered γ-phase (L1_2 structure), where Al atoms exclusively occupy the corner positions while Fe and Mn atoms reside at the face-centered positions. Panel (c) illustrates the j-carbide (κ-carbide) with ideal E2_1 perovskite structure and stoichiometry (Fe,Mn)3AlC, where carbon occupies the body-center octahedral interstitial site coordinated by Fe and Mn. The sequence demonstrates the progressive ordering from random solid solution to L1_2 short-range order and finally to the fully ordered j-carbide. The caption notes that j-carbide forms when Al exceeds 2 wt% and can exist as a metastable non-stoichiometric (Fe,Mn)3AlCx (x<1) phase via spinodal decomposition. This figure supports the core concept that ordering and carbide precipitation govern the strengthening mechanisms in low-density steels.

Figure 7 presents isothermal phase sections of Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys at 900 °C plotted as C (wt%) versus Al (wt%) for fixed manganese contents of (a) 10 wt%, (b) 20 wt%, and (c) 30 wt%. Each panel compares experimentally determined phase boundaries—red lines and individual alloy composition points from references [102,105]—against thermodynamic calculations from FactSage 6.4 (solid black lines) and a CALPHAD approach (dotted lines [107]). The experimental data symbols denote the located phase fields as reported by the caption: open circles for γ, open triangles for (γ+κ), open squares for (γ+M₃C), solid squares for (α+κ), and solid diamonds for (c+a). As Mn increases from 10% to 30%, the single γ austenite region changes in extent and the κ-carbide stability fields shift toward lower Al and C contents; notably, the 30% Mn section exhibits a (γ+M₇C₃+κ) phase field not present at lower Mn levels. The calculated boundaries generally follow experimental trends but deviate in specific high-Al or high-C regions. These diagrams define the equilibrium phase constitution—including austenite, ferrite (α), κ-carbide, M₃C, M₇C₃, and B2 phases—and thereby provide the thermodynamic basis for alloy design and processing window selection in low-density steels.

Figure 8 presents two calculated equilibrium phase-fraction diagrams plotting phase percentage versus temperature for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, with calculations performed using FactSage 6.4 software as stated in the caption. Subfigure (a) shows alloy Fe-13.5Mn-6.3Al-0.78C, where ferrite (α) dominates at low temperatures (~85% at 400–550°C) and progressively transforms to austenite (γ) reaching 100% above ~780°C. This alloy exhibits a κ-carbide peak near 8% around 600–650°C, minor M23C6 at lower temperatures, and transient M7C3 between ~550 and 650°C. Subfigure (b) depicts alloy Fe-18Mn-7Al-0.85C, displaying a broader κ-carbide stability window with a higher maximum fraction of ~13–14% and full γ-stabilization above ~750°C, alongside M23C6 at temperatures below ~500°C. The side-by-side comparison reveals that higher Mn and Al contents expand the austenite temperature window and promote greater κ-carbide precipitation, providing thermodynamic guidance for designing precipitation-strengthened or duplex lightweight steels.

Figure 9 presents three polythermal sections of the Fe-15Mn-xAl-yC phase diagram calculated using FactSage, showing phase equilibria as a function of carbon content (0–2.5 wt%) at a fixed 15 wt% Mn and varying aluminum contents. The three panels correspond to (a) Al = 4%, (b) Al = 7%, and (d) Al = 10%. The vertical axis spans 200–1600 °C and the horizontal axis spans 0–2.5 wt% C. Each subfigure delineates distinct phase fields including liquid (L), austenite (γ), delta-ferrite (δ/α), and various carbides such as M₂₃C₆, M₇C₃, and M₃C. As Al increases, the phase stability shifts dramatically: the 4% Al section is dominated by austenite and cementite-type carbides at low temperatures; the 7% Al section introduces B2-ordered regions and κ-carbide phase fields alongside M₂₃C₆ and M₇C₃; while the 10% Al section shows a drastically simplified low-temperature region dominated by B2+κ and α(δ)+κ, with carbide phases largely suppressed. Dash-dotted lines mark the formation temperature of the j phase, and dashed lines indicate the disorder-to-order transition temperature. These calculations illustrate that increasing Al content strongly stabilizes ordered intermetallic phases and κ-carbide while destabilizing conventional M₇C₃/M₂₃C₆ carbides, thereby governing the microstructural design space for lightweight Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.

The figure presents two polythermal sections of calculated phase diagrams for quaternary Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys obtained using FactSage. Panel (a) maps phase stability against Al content (0–12 wt%) at fixed Mn = 20 wt% and C = 0.85 wt%, while panel (b) maps stability against Mn content (0–30 wt%) at fixed Al = 7 wt% and C = 0.85 wt%. Both sections delineate liquid (L), austenite (γ), delta-ferrite (δ), alpha-ferrite (α), ordered B2, κ-carbide, and cementite-type carbides including M₃C, M₇C₃, and M₂₃C₆. The dash-dotted lines mark the formation temperature of the j phase, and the dashed lines indicate the disorder-to-order transition temperature. Visually, increasing Al strongly stabilizes δ-ferrite and promotes B2 and κ-phase fields, whereas increasing Mn widens the γ-austenite domain and suppresses high-temperature δ formation. These computed phase boundaries serve as a thermodynamic framework for predicting microstructural evolution, solidification paths, and precipitation behavior during processing of low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.

Figure 11 presents a schematic process-flow diagram illustrating five distinct thermomechanical processing routes (labeled 1 through 5) for manufacturing austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel strips, covering both hot-rolled (HR) and cold-rolled (CR) product variants. The left side depicts hot rolling reheated to 1100–1200°C with a finish rolling temperature (FRT) of 900°C, followed by divergent cooling paths: Route 1 (dashed line) indicates direct fast cooling to an intermediate temperature and subsequent slow cooling or isothermal holding, while Route 2 shows fast cooling to room temperature followed by isothermal annealing. The center-right side illustrates cold-rolled processing, where strip undergoes cold rolling (CR) followed either by continuous annealing at 600–900°C for non-age-hardenable grades, or by solution treatment at 900–1100°C in the single-austenite region and quenching for age-hardenable alloys. Routes 3, 4, and 5 denote aging variants after solution treatment: Route 3 proceeds directly to aging without isothermal holding, whereas Routes 4 and 5 include isothermal holds intended to maximize age-hardening effects via κ′-carbide precipitation, commonly performed at 500–700°C for 5–20 h. The diagram emphasizes that post-hot-rolling cooling rates must be sufficiently high to avoid intergranular κ-carbide formation, and that process selection depends on whether the target microstructure requires precipitation hardening or recovery/recrystallization annealing.

Figure 13 presents a Time-Temperature-Transformation (TTT) diagram illustrating the isothermal decomposition of the γ-solid solution in a Fe-28Mn-8.5Al-1C-1.25Si alloy over 400–950 °C and 0.01–1000 h. The diagram displays five distinct reaction zones bounded by C-shaped transformation-start curves. Area 1 (γ) corresponds to spinodal decomposition. Area 2 (γ+κ′) represents homogeneous matrix κ0-carbide precipitation. Area 3 (γ+κ′+κ*) marks grain boundary κ*-carbide formation. Area 4 (γ+κ′+κ*+α(B2/DO3)) denotes a discontinuous grain boundary reaction product of α(B2/DO3). Area 5 (γ+κ+B2/DO3) contains the equilibrium phases. The right-hand side labels indicate the ordered intermetallic regions: B2, B2+DO3, and DO3. The curves show that higher temperatures near 900–950 °C accelerate initial transformations, whereas aging below 500 °C significantly prolongs the incubation time for grain boundary κ* precipitation. This TTT diagram establishes the full precipitation sequence and demonstrates that Si addition suppresses β-Mn while promoting B2/DO3 formation, guiding aging process design for these low-density steels.

This figure presents a set of three secondary electron SEM images of an Fe-18Mn-7Al-0.85C austenitic steel isothermally held at 700°C for various durations, illustrating the morphological evolution of κ-carbide precipitation and cellular transformation at austenite grain boundaries. Panel (a), captured at ×20,000 after 1 h, reveals fine needle-like precipitates within the austenite matrix and the initial formation of a lamellar product along a grain boundary. Panel (b), at ×10,000 after 25 h, displays a well-developed lamellar colony composed of austenite (γ), ferrite (α), and κ-carbide (κ) growing from the grain boundary into the matrix. Panel (c), at ×2,000 after 100 h, shows the extensive propagation and coarsening of these lamellar colonies at a lower magnification. The caption states that the images track the growing feature of κ-carbides and the evolution of κ*-carbide within (κ + γ + α) lamellar colonies. Citation text reports that this cellular transformation is a discontinuous reaction involving simultaneous nucleation of κ*-carbide and α-ferrite at grain boundaries, which grow cooperatively as lamellar nodules into austenite, often accompanied by austenite twins in the lamellar austenite grains.

Figure 15 presents the microstructures of a Fe-13.5Mn-6.3Al-0.78C steel after solution treatment at 1000 °C followed by aging at 600 °C for 100 h [108]. Panel (a) is an optical microscope image (scale bar 100 μm) showing large j-pearlite colonies distributed along austenite grain boundaries. Panels (b) and (c) are TEM bright field images (scale bar 0.2 μm) revealing the internal lamellar structures within various j-pearlites. The labels in (b) and (c) identify α (ferrite), κ (kappa/j-carbide), and C (M23C6 carbide), forming a characteristic eutectoid lamellar morphology. According to the caption and citation, these lamellae represent the product phases from the decomposition of retained austenite via a eutectoid reaction: γ → α + κ + M23C6. Because the Al concentration exceeds the eutectoid composition, proeutectoid ferrite and j-carbide precipitate in austenite prior to the eutectoid reaction, and the remaining austenite subsequently decomposes into the three-phase lamellar aggregate. The figure collectively demonstrates the hierarchical structure of j-pearlite from grain-boundary colonies down to nanoscale lamellae, supporting the phase transformation mechanism in aged Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels.

Figure 16 presents a composition-phase map for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels illustrating the precipitation of intragranular κ′-carbide (j0-carbide) as a function of alloy composition, with Al (wt%) on the horizontal axis and C (wt%) on the vertical axis. Open circles denote alloys in the single γ-austenite phase field, solid squares indicate the (γ + κ) two-phase field, and solid diamonds mark the (γ + α + κ) three-phase field. The solid lines labeled 15Mn, 20Mn, and 30Mn represent the κ-formation boundaries predicted by Eq. (3) from Huang et al., showing that higher Mn content shifts the boundary toward lower Al and C contents. Dashed and dash-dotted lines demarcate the γ + α phase boundaries for 15Mn and 30Mn alloys, respectively. The experimental data confirm that intragranular κ-carbide precipitation is favored at higher Al and C contents, while increasing Mn expands the γ + κ stability region. This figure supports the core conclusion that alloy composition can be designed to control κ-carbide precipitation and thereby tailor the microstructure and mechanical properties of austenitic lightweight steels.

The figure comprises two phase stability diagrams illustrating the effects of Al and Mn contents on the solvus of κ-carbides (denoted as j-carbides in the caption) in Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, adapted from [128]. Subfigure (a) for Fe-30Mn-xAl-1C alloys plots temperature versus Al content (5–10.5 wt%), delineating three phase fields: γ (austenite), γ+κ* (austenite with intergranular κ-carbide), and γ+κ*+κ' (austenite with both intergranular and intragranular κ-carbides). Subfigure (b) for Fe-xMn-7Al-1C alloys shows the corresponding boundaries against Mn content (23–29 wt%). Solid lines trace the intergranular κ-carbide solvus, while dashed lines indicate the onset of intragranular κ'-carbide precipitation. Experimental symbols (crosses, open circles, and filled circles) mark the observed phase boundaries. Collectively, the diagrams reveal that Al additions significantly elevate the solvus temperatures of both intergranular and intragranular κ-carbides, whereas Mn additions cause only a slight reduction. These trends establish the compositional windows for single-phase austenite versus carbide-stabilized microstructures.

This figure pairs a scanning electron micrograph (a) with a formation-mechanism schematic (b) to document B2 intermetallic precipitation in a Fe–15Mn–10Al–0.86C–5Ni steel after 70% cold-rolling thickness reduction and annealing at 900 °C for 2 min. Micrograph (a) displays a multiphase microstructure at 10 μm scale with three annotated zones: area (1) shows continuous B2 stringer bands transformed from δ-ferrite that the caption reports was produced from the liquid; area (2) reveals coarser B2 particles (200–1000 nm) transformed from α-precipitates situated at prior austenite (γ) grain boundaries and edges; and area (3) reveals finer B2 particles (50–300 nm) transformed from α-precipitates decorating shear bands inside γ grains. Schematic (b) explains these distributions through two divergent annealing pathways after cold rolling: one where deformed γ grains (10–20 μm) recrystallize into fine grains (1–2 μm) and promote B2 precipitation at boundaries (type 2), and another where no recrystallization occurs so that B2 precipitates preferentially form along retained shear bands (type 3). The figure supports the central argument that the size and site of B2 precipitates are dictated by recrystallization behavior, and that nanoscale B2 particles can strengthen austenitic steels if their precipitation can be controlled, though a robust strategy to achieve such nanoscale dispersions remains unclear.

Figure 19 presents paired micrographs demonstrating phase transformations and interfacial reactions during hot working in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. Subfigure (a) is an optical microstructure (scale bar 10 μm) of a Fe-5Mn-6Al-0.4C steel; the inset distinguishes δ-ferrite bands from γ-phase bands, while the main image highlights α-ferrite formed at hot-rolling temperatures within the γ band, together with α+κ phases formed at coiling temperatures. Subfigure (b) is an SEM micrograph (scale bar 10 μm, magnification 1.46 K×, EHT 20.00 kV) of a Fe-2Mn-8Al-0.2C steel showing a shell structure in which columnar κ-carbide precipitates envelop an austenite (γ) island along the δ-ferrite boundary on the α-ferrite matrix. Collectively, the images evidence that element partitioning between ferrite and austenite during hot rolling drives either γ→α transformation or direct κ-carbide formation at phase boundaries depending on alloy composition; lower C and Mn contents favor high-temperature austenite decomposition into α-ferrite and κ-carbide, whereas increasing Mn suppresses coarse κ-carbide formation during hot working.

Figure 20 presents microstructures of an air-cooled hot-rolled Fe–9Mn–6Al–0.15C alloy specimen. Panel (a) is an optical micrograph (scale bar 100 μm) with the rolling direction indicated, showing partially recrystallized δ-ferrite (labeled δ), γ-austenite (labeled γ), and lath-like gray regions marked α′+α+γ. Panel (b) is a higher-magnification SEM micrograph (scale bar 10 μm) revealing internal details within the lath-like phase, consisting of γ-austenite (labeled γ), lath-like α′-martensite (labeled α′), small globular α-ferrite particles (labeled α, indicated by white arrows), and coarse δ-ferrite regions (labeled δ). The accompanying text reports that in Fe-(9–12)Mn-6Al-(0.15–0.3)C alloys, increasing Mn above 6% stabilizes austenite during air cooling, suppressing γ→α+κ decomposition so that austenite transforms to martensite and some retained austenite (γ_R) remains at room temperature; no κ-carbide precipitates are observed, and the amount of α′-martensite decreases with increasing Mn or C content.

This figure comprises two line graphs presenting the mechanical response of binary Fe-Al steels as a function of aluminum content (0-25 wt%) across ordered and disordered crystal structures. Panel (a) displays tensile data: yield strength (YS, blue diamonds), ultimate tensile strength (UTS, open circles), total elongation (TE, red triangles), and uniform elongation (UE, black triangles) plotted against Al content, divided by vertical dashed lines into phase fields A2, K1, A2+DO3, DO3, and B2. YS increases with Al solid solution up to ~13 wt%, peaks near 575 MPa, then drops sharply between 13-15 wt% before rising again gradually, whereas UTS increases monotonically. TE declines from ~35% to ~15% as Al increases, with a ductile-to-brittle transition reported around 8-10 wt% Al. Panel (b) presents the work hardening rate measured between 1-1.5% plastic strain versus Al content. The rate stays low (<2 GPa) in the disordered A2 regime below ~7% Al, climbs steeply to a sharp maximum near 16 wt% Al within the DO3 field (~16 GPa), and subsequently falls to moderate levels above 20 wt% Al. Collectively, the data show that Al provides solid solution strengthening in ferrite, but the formation of ordered intermetallic phases (DO3/B2) fundamentally alters strain hardening through superdislocation-mediated deformation while degrading tensile ductility.

Figure 22 presents typical engineering stress-strain curves for austenitic and austenite-based duplex Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. Subfigure (a) shows the effect of Al and C contents on solution-treated (1000°C/1 h, water-quenched) steels, including 20Mn-10Al-1.5C, 20Mn-11Al-1.0C, 20Mn-10Al-1.0C [8], and 22Mn-(0, 3, 6)Al-0.6C [72]. Visually, the Al-free 22Mn-0Al-0.6C curve displays higher strain hardening than the Al-alloyed grades, while increasing Al and C generally raises yield strength but reduces strain hardening capacity and total elongation. Subfigure (b) displays curves for 18Mn-10Al-0.9C-5Ni annealed at 900°C [22], 28Mn-12Al-1.0C in WQ and aged states (550°C/5 min, 46 min) [6], and 28Mn-10Al-1.0C (WQ) [74]. The caption and cited text report that Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C achieves total elongation approaching 100% via microband-induced plasticity (MBIP), whereas 28Mn-12Al-1C is an austenite-based duplex steel containing about 8% δ-ferrite and nano-sized κ-carbides, giving higher yield strength but lower elongation (~65%). Aging increases strength yet degrades strain hardenability and ductility, and highly alloyed grades can exhibit near-horizontal or even negative work-hardening rates. Collectively, the curves support the core conclusion that deformation mechanisms evolve from TWIP toward planar glide, MBIP, or shear-band-induced plasticity as Al and C contents increase.

This figure simultaneously plots room-temperature true stress (lower rising curves) and strain hardening rate (upper curves) versus true strain for several Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. The legend identifies six compositions: Fe-22Mn-0.6C [83], Fe-30Mn-2Al-1.2C [80], Fe-30Mn-8Al-1.2C [85], Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C [74], Fe-27Mn-12Al-0.9C [6], and Fe-15Mn-9Al-0.7C [6]. The upper curves illustrate distinct strain-hardening behaviors: the TWIP steel (Fe-22Mn-0.6C) displays the characteristic 'hump' linked to deformation twinning, while the MBIP (Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C), DSBR (Fe-30Mn-8Al-1.2C), and SIP (Fe-27Mn-12Al-0.9C) alloys exhibit qualitatively similar strain dependencies despite operating via planar glide, short-range orders, or kappa-prime carbides rather than twinning. The Fe-15Mn-9Al-0.7C alloy shows a monotonic decay typical of conventional alloys. As the accompanying text states, the hump magnitude decreases and flattens with increased aging, and none of the proposed mechanisms fully accounts for negative strain hardening observed in highly alloyed or peak-aged conditions.

Fig. 24 presents a three-dimensional composition-dependent stacking fault energy (SFE) map for Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels at room temperature. Subfigure (a) displays SFE surfaces as functions of carbon (0–1.2 wt%) and manganese (0–50 wt%) at fixed aluminum levels of 3, 5, 8, and 11 wt%, while (b) shows SFE variations with aluminum (1–12 wt%) and manganese at fixed carbon contents of 0, 0.3, 0.6, 0.9, 1.2, and 1.5 wt%. Both plots reveal that increasing Al and C strongly raises the SFE, whereas Mn initially decreases SFE before slightly increasing it at higher contents. Subfigure (c) translates these SFE data into a 3D deformation mechanism map delineating phase fields for TRIP, TWIP, MBIP, and MBIP plus kappa-carbide precipitation, bounded by iso-SFE surfaces at 20 and 80 mJ/m² and a kappa-carbide forming border. The figure demonstrates that rising Al content progressively shifts the deformation mechanism from TRIP to TWIP and then to MBIP, enabling predictive alloy design via thermodynamic SFE calculations.

This figure presents a mechanical property map plotting total elongation (TE) versus ultimate tensile strength (UTS) for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels alongside conventional steel grades, using uniform ASTM E-8 specimen geometry (50 mm by 12.5 mm by 1 mm). The tensile data were compiled from solution-treated and water-quenched strips reported in multiple literature sources. The scatter plot categorizes steels into six microstructural regimes represented by colored ellipses: δ single phase (including IF, Mild, BH, CMn, HSLA), δ-based duplex, TRIP/DP/CP, γ single phase (Mn-TWIP), γ-based duplex, and martensitic steels (MS) containing γ+(δ)+coarse κ. The x-axis spans 0–1800 MPa for UTS, the y-axis spans 0–100% for TE, and a top axis indicates the strength-ductility product (UTS×TE) in GPa%, with diagonal contour lines marking constant product values. Mn-TWIP steels occupy the upper-right region with UTS ~600–1000 MPa and TE ~60–95%, while conventional δ single-phase ferritic steels cluster in the lower-left with moderate strength and ductility. MS grades exhibit high strength (>1000 MPa) but poor ductility (<10%). Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys primarily distribute within the γ single-phase and γ-based duplex fields, demonstrating that austenitic lightweight steels achieve exceptional strength-ductility balances competitive with conventional advanced high-strength steels.

Figure 26 displays engineering stress–strain curves for two ferritic steels, Fe-6.8Al and Fe-8.1Al (850°C/5 min [41], red curves), and two ferrite-based duplex steels, Fe-3.3Mn-6.6Al-0.2C [44] (blue curves) and Fe-8.5Mn-5.6Al-0.3C [51] (black curves), after different heat treatments. The ferritic steels show the lowest strength, with ultimate tensile strength (UTS) around 450–500 MPa and total elongation (TE) of roughly 15–30%. The Fe-3.3Mn-6.6Al-0.2C steel exhibits moderate strength and enhanced elongation when austempered at 400°C/10 min after 800°C/5 min, which the caption attributes to a microstructure mixture of δ-ferrite, bainite, martensite, and retained austenite. The Fe-8.5Mn-5.6Al-0.3C steel achieves the highest performance, reaching approximately 850 MPa UTS and up to about 70% strain under 800°C/1 min, 800°C/30 min, and 900°C/30 min annealing. The text reports that the TRIP mechanism is operative in the 800°C-annealed samples, whereas simultaneous TRIP and TWIP occur in the 900°C/30 min condition, enabling these duplex steels to deliver a superior strength–ductility balance of 600–950 MPa tensile strength and 30–70% total elongation.

This figure presents the tensile properties of two series of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels as a function of Al content (0–12 wt%). Subfigure (a) shows Fe-30Mn-1.2C-xAl and subfigure (b) shows Fe-30Mn-0.4C-xAl, both solutionized at 1100°C for 2 h. In each subfigure, the upper panel plots ultimate tensile strength (UTS, square markers) and yield strength (YS, diamond markers) in MPa, while the lower panel plots total elongation (TE, triangle markers) and δ (cross or inverted-triangle markers) in percent. Solid curves denote water-quenched samples and dotted curves denote water-quenched plus aged samples (550°C for 24 h). For the high-carbon series (a), UTS and YS remain relatively stable up to ~7% Al but increase sharply at higher Al contents, especially in the aged condition due to κ′-carbide precipitation hardening; TE peaks around 10% Al. For the low-carbon series (b), adding 2% Al produces a fully austenitic microstructure with minimum strength but maximum TE, while further Al addition increases δ-ferrite fraction, raising strength but reducing ductility. Aging significantly strengthens the high-Al high-C alloys but barely affects the low-C duplex alloys.

Figure 28 comprises two line-graph panels showing how carbon content influences the tensile properties and phase constitution of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels: (a) Fe-30Mn-9Al-xC and (b) Fe-20Mn-10Al-xC. In each panel, the left y-axis records strength (MPa) for ultimate tensile strength (UTS, magenta squares) and yield strength (YS, blue diamonds), while the right y-axis records total elongation (TE, black triangles) and δ-ferrite fraction (δ, red crosses) versus C content (0–2 wt%). Visual trends show that δ-ferrite decreases monotonically with increasing C for both series, vanishing near the single-phase austenite transition points that the accompanying text reports as ~0.7 wt% C for the 30Mn-9Al alloy and ~1.0 wt% C for the 20Mn-10Al alloy. In the duplex regime, YS stays relatively flat or decreases slightly and TE rises as austenite volume increases; after transitioning to single-phase austenite, both UTS and YS climb steeply with C while TE drops. The text attributes the duplex-stage behavior to reduced Al solid-solution hardening and early interfacial failure at δ/γ boundaries, and attributes the single-phase-stage ductility loss to increased κ-carbide precipitation or short-range ordering upon cooling. Collectively, the figure establishes carbon as the critical lever controlling the δ/γ phase balance and the ensuing strength–ductility trade-off in these steels.

This figure comprises two mechanical-property panels showing the compositional dependence of strength, ductility, and impact toughness in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. Panel (a) plots yield strength (YS), ultimate tensile strength (UTS), total elongation (TE), reduction of area (δ), and U-notch Charpy impact energy (E_CUN) versus Al content (3–11 wt%) for Fe-30Mn-xAl-(0.85,0.95)C alloys that were water-quenched from 1150 °C and aged at 550 °C for 16 h. Panel (b) shows the same property set as a function of C content (0.3–1.2 wt%) for Fe-30Mn-9Al-xC steels water-quenched from 1050 °C (dashed curves) and aged at 550 °C for 16 h (solid curves). In (a), UTS and YS remain low and flat below approximately 7 wt% Al, then rise markedly at higher Al, whereas E_CUN and TE peak around 6–7 wt% Al and decrease thereafter due to κ-carbide formation and the introduction of δ-ferrite. In (b), increasing C up to about 0.7 wt% simultaneously increases strength, TE, and impact toughness; above this level, strength continues to climb but ductility and toughness drop because of increased κ-precipitates. Collectively, the data indicate that pronounced age-hardening and a balanced strength-ductility-toughness combination require Al above 7 wt% and an optimal C content near 0.7 wt%.

This figure is a comparative logarithmic plot displaying precipitation strengthening magnitude versus precipitation fraction for carbides in microalloyed and Fe-Mn-Al-C steels. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 500 MPa, while the horizontal axis spans precipitation fraction from 0.0001 to 1 on a logarithmic scale. Four phases are compared as vertical range bars: NbC spans roughly 70–180 MPa at fractions near 0.0003–0.001; VC spans approximately 0–125 MPa near 0.001; TiC exhibits the largest range of about 120–450 MPa around 0.001–0.003; and κ-carbide covers roughly 100–350 MPa at higher fractions visually near 0.1–0.3. Accompanying text reports that the κ-phase strengthening potential is 100–350 MPa for a volume fraction of approximately 0.05–0.35. The chart illustrates that κ-carbides in austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steels deliver substantial precipitation strengthening comparable to TiC, but require significantly higher volume fractions, highlighting their distinctive age-hardening behavior in low-density steels.

Figure 31 presents aging curves and tensile property evolution for austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. Panel (a) plots Brinell hardness versus aging time for Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C-0.35Si [120] aged at 450°C, 500°C, 550°C and 600°C, showing that higher temperatures accelerate aging kinetics and shorten the time to peak hardness. Panel (b) compares hardness curves of several alloys aged at 550°C—Fe-34Mn-11Al-0.98C [120], Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C [120], Fe-31Mn-7.8Al-0.88C [120], Fe-29Mn-4.9Al-0.98C [120], Fe-34Mn-7.1Al-0.5C [120] and Fe-30Mn-7.8Al-1.3C [118]—demonstrating that increased Al and C contents strengthen the age-hardening response. Panel (c) shows UTS, YS and total elongation (TE) versus aging time for Fe-28Mn-9Al-1.8C at 450°C [77], Fe-30Mn-8.5Al-2.0C at 550°C [78] and Fe-30Mn-7.8Al-1.3C at 550°C [118]; UTS and YS rise to maxima then decline, whereas TE decreases progressively. The figure supports that peak strength requires prolonged aging, yet a broad range of strength-ductility combinations can be realized via processing control.

The figure presents engineering stress–strain curves for two Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels under various cold-rolling and annealing conditions. Subfigure (a) shows tensile properties of a Fe-22.5Mn-1.2Al-0.3C steel [152] after cold rolling reductions of 30% and 50% (CR30% and CR50%), and after subsequent annealing treatments including CR30%+630°C/10m, CR50%+550°C/30m, CR30%+700°C/15m, and CR50%+700°C/10m. The as-cold-rolled curves exhibit high yield strengths near 900–1000 MPa but limited elongation of approximately 20%. Annealing at 630°C after 30% CR reduces the yield strength while moderately improving ductility, whereas annealing at 700°C substantially lowers the yield strength to roughly 300–400 MPa and markedly increases elongation to about 60–65%. Subfigure (b) displays tensile curves of a Fe-22Mn-7Al-0.8C steel [5] subjected to 50% cold rolling followed by recrystallization annealing at temperatures from 800°C to 975°C for durations of 1–25 min. Lower annealing temperatures and shorter times (e.g., 800°C/1 min and 850°C/1 min) yield the highest tensile strengths exceeding 1000 MPa with elongation around 35%, while higher temperatures and longer annealing (e.g., 975°C/25 min) significantly reduce strength to approximately 760 MPa but maximize elongation to roughly 50%. Collectively, these curves demonstrate that post-cold-rolling annealing parameters govern the degree of recrystallization and austenitic grain refinement, enabling a tunable strength–ductility balance in austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.

This figure plots Charpy V-notch (CVN) impact energy versus test temperature for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, with shaded bands indicating typical impact-energy ranges for austenitic, duplex, ferritic, and martensitic stainless steels. Curve 1 (solid line with open squares) shows as-solutionized Fe-28Mn-5Al-1C treated at 1050°C for 75 min (grain size d = 41 μm); curve 2 (solid line with open squares) shows the same alloy solutionized at 1050°C for 8 h (d = 135 μm); curve 3 (red line) shows as-solutionized Fe-30Mn-10Al-1C-1Si treated at 1050°C for 1 h (d = 120 μm); curve 4 (blue dashed line with squares) shows Fe-28Mn-5Al-1C solutionized and aged at 550°C for 16 h; and curve 5 (red dashed line with diamonds) shows Fe-30Mn-10Al-1C-1Si solutionized and aged at 550°C for 16 h. Curves 1–3 exhibit high impact energies comparable to austenitic stainless steels at room temperature, but undergo a ductile-to-brittle transition below approximately −100°C, with the finer-grained curve 1 maintaining higher low-temperature toughness than the coarser-grained curve 2. After aging, curve 4 displays drastically reduced impact energy that falls continuously with decreasing temperature, while curve 5 reports near-zero toughness across the tested range. The caption and citation text state that the formation of j-carbide (kappa-carbide) precipitates during aging is primarily responsible for this severe embrittlement.

This scatter chart compares the specific energy absorption (E_spec, J/mm³) of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels against conventional deep-drawing steels (IF steel, HC300LA, and DC04) as a function of aluminum content (wt%) at a crash-relevant deformation rate of 10³/s. The conventional steels, indicated by open symbols in the legend, exhibit low E_spec values between approximately 0.16 and 0.25 J/mm³. In contrast, Fe-Mn-Al-C steels (green diamonds) show strongly elevated energy absorption: at ~6.3 wt% Al, values scatter between ~0.27 and 0.34 J/mm³, while at Al contents above 7 wt%, E_spec stabilizes around ~0.5 J/mm³ (ranging from ~0.43 to 0.56 J/mm³), comparable to Mn TWIP steels and roughly 50% higher than conventional grades. The caption and surrounding text attribute this enhanced energy absorption to severe shear band formation at high strain rates, specifically adiabatic shear band (ASB) formation, where localized deformation creates momentary high temperatures that cannot be readily dissipated in these low-thermal-conductivity alloys.

This figure presents cyclic stress-response curves (stress amplitude versus number of cycles, log scale) from strain-controlled fatigue tests on two Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. The dotted curves show an austenitic Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C alloy [161] after three aging treatments—550 °C/24 h, 710 °C/2 h, and 550 °C/2 h—tested at a fixed total strain amplitude of 0.8 %. All three conditions exhibit continuous cyclic softening; the 550 °C/24 h condition starts near 1050 MPa and declines to about 750 MPa, whereas the 710 °C/2 h and 550 °C/2 h conditions begin near 750 MPa and 650 MPa, respectively, and soften toward roughly 450–500 MPa. The solid curves show a duplex Fe-30Mn-10Al-0.4C alloy [162] with an austenite-plus-ferrite microstructure tested at total strain amplitudes of 0.2 %, 0.4 %, 1.0 %, 1.5 %, and 2.0 %. At low strain amplitudes (0.2 %–0.4 %) the alloy undergoes cyclic softening, while at higher amplitudes (1.0 %–2.0 %) it first exhibits cyclic strengthening to a peak stress amplitude before subsequent softening. The figure supports that cyclic softening in the aged austenitic alloy is linked to planar slip, shearing and mechanical dissolution of precipitates, and persistent slip band formation, whereas the duplex alloy displays strain-amplitude-dependent cyclic hardening/softening behavior with fatigue crack initiation reported in ferrite.
